overview.introduction: Namespaces provide a mechanism for isolating groups of resources within a single cluster.
overview.why_it_matters: Understanding Namespaces is critical for configuring resilient container cloud architectures.
overview.industry_usage: Used daily by platform developers to scale APIs, isolate systems, and ensure high availability.
overview.what_you_will_build: A series of configured Kubernetes resources verified inside a K3s sandbox environment.
learning_outcomes: Verify workload status using kubectl commands.
Enforce best practices for configurations security.

key_concepts.title: Resource Isolation
key_concepts.definition: Namespaces divide cluster resources among multiple users or projects, preventing naming collisions and enforcing access limits.
key_concepts.explanation: When configuring Namespaces, declarative state configurations ensure consistent runtimes.

commands.name: kubectl get
commands.output_explanation: Shows standard resource listing from the current namespace.

commands.name: kubectl describe
commands.purpose: Get detailed attributes of a specific resource.
commands.output_explanation: Shows events, configuration metadata, status details, and environment variables.

key_concepts.pro_tip: Always use declarative YAML manifests rather than command-line triggers for managing Namespaces.
key_concepts.best_practices: Specify explicit namespaces for all resources to avoid cluttering the default space.
key_concepts.common_mistakes: Forgetting to verify current kubectl contexts before executing delete commands.
key_concepts.real_world_usage: Organizations automate cluster state rollouts using GitOps operators (like ArgoCD).
key_concepts.summary: Namespaces provides the base layer for robust container operations at scale.

interactive_examples.scenario: Confirm the status of your workloads inside the current namespace.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Automated status checks inside DevOps build pipelines.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Executing command without active network connection to master node API.
interactive_examples.tips: Add '-o wide' to see pod IPs and node assignments.

lessons.title: 1. Initial Check (Create Namespace)
lessons.explanation: Verifies configuration states from master nodes.
lessons.instruction: Create a namespace named 'devlab-test' to isolate testing resources.

lessons.title: 2. Verify Details
lessons.explanation: Ensure the resource was created correctly.
lessons.instruction: Inspect resources details using kubectl describe.

lessons.title: 3. Check Logs
lessons.explanation: Provides troubleshooting traces for container runtimes.
lessons.instruction: Print container logs to console screen.

lessons.title: 4. Create temporary resource
lessons.explanation: Run test scripts or check API connectivity.
lessons.instruction: Start a temporary debug pod running busybox.

lessons.title: 5. List Services
lessons.explanation: Find active service IPs mapping ports.
lessons.instruction: Retrieve list of services using get.

lessons.title: 6. Clean temporary resource
lessons.explanation: Wipe out test pods to recover namespace capacity.
lessons.instruction: Remove the temporary debug pod named tmp-debug.

lessons.title: 7. Get All Workloads
lessons.explanation: Queries pods, services, deployments, and replica sets at once.
lessons.instruction: List all active components in default namespace namespaces.

lessons.title: Mini Challenge: Deploy in Namespace
lessons.definition: No step-by-step instructions are provided here. Execute this task directly.
lessons.explanation: Complete this task on your own.
lessons.instruction: Deploy a pod named 'ns-pod' running 'nginx:alpine' inside the 'devlab-test' namespace.
lessons.hint: Run command: kubectl run ns-pod --image=nginx:alpine -n devlab-test

exercises.title: Deploy Namespaces manifest
exercises.problem: Practice declaring resource layouts by querying node configurations status.

quiz.question: What is the primary function of Kubernetes Namespaces?
quiz.explanation: Kubernetes automates the deployment and management of containerized stacks.

quiz.question: Which CLI command is used to interact with Kubernetes clusters?
quiz.explanation: kubectl is the official command line client interface for K8s clusters.

quiz.question: What is K3s?
options: A highly lightweight, certified Kubernetes distribution.
A package manager for database services.
quiz.answer: A highly lightweight, certified Kubernetes distribution.
quiz.explanation: K3s is a minimal Kubernetes footprint packaged as a single binary, optimized for local and edge setups.

quiz.question: How do you specify a namespace filter in kubectl commands?
quiz.explanation: Use the '-n' (or '--namespace') flag option to specify target resources.

quiz.question: Which of the following is the control plane component of Kubernetes?
quiz.explanation: The API server (kube-apiserver) is the central entry point that exposes the K8s API.

quiz.question: What stores the state and configuration data of a Kubernetes cluster?
quiz.explanation: etcd is a consistent, highly-available key-value store used as Kubernetes' backing store.

quiz.question: What runs on worker nodes to monitor container health and lifecycle?
quiz.explanation: The kubelet agent runs on each node in the cluster and makes sure that containers are running in a Pod.

quiz.question: Which command displays details of a resource named 'web-service'?
quiz.explanation: kubectl describe displays detailed metadata, events, and specs of target resources.

quiz.question: What is a YAML file used for in Kubernetes?
quiz.explanation: YAML configurations declare the desired state of API objects, allowing version control and repeatability.

quiz.question: What happens if a pod crashes in a Deployment?
options: Kubernetes automatically schedules a new pod to replace it.
The admin must rebuild the image.
quiz.answer: Kubernetes automatically schedules a new pod to replace it.
quiz.explanation: Deployment controllers continuously reconcile active states to match desired states (self-healing).

resources.summary: Learn more about Kubernetes Namespaces through official documentation, guides, and manuals.

links.title: Kubernetes Namespaces Documentation Guide
links.description: Official documentation and concepts manual for Namespaces.

links.title: Interactive Kubernetes Playground
links.description: Browser-based sandbox environment for testing kubectl.

links.title: Kubernetes Cheat Sheet
links.description: Quick reference guide for common kubectl control commands.

resources.cheat_sheet:
kubectl get pods -n <ns>        # List pods
kubectl describe pod <name>    # Describe pod
kubectl apply -f <file>        # Apply manifest
kubectl delete pod <name>      # Delete pod

commands_table.name: kubectl get
commands_table.description: List resources in the current namespace context.

commands_table.name: kubectl apply
commands_table.description: Create or update resources using a YAML manifest file.

revision_notes: Kubernetes is declarative; you define the desired state and the control loop resolves it.
Always run kubectl commands with -n to ensure isolation.
best_practices: Use declarative configuration files rather than imperative commands.
Organize resource environments inside dedicated namespaces.
Specify resource requests and limits for memory and CPU.
beginner_mistakes: Deploying resources into default namespaces instead of structured namespace divisions.
Typos in YAML key indentations causing parser validation crashes.

interview_questions.question: What is Kubernetes Namespaces?
interview_questions.answer: It represents the resource configuration model for Namespaces in Kubernetes API namespaces.

books.title: Kubernetes Up & Running — Kelsey Hightower
books.description: Excellent entry guide to Kubernetes clusters operations.
